title: Practical Play & Avoiding Blunders
subtitle: "The blunders are all there on the board, waiting to be made." — Savielly Tartakower

blocks.html:
Not all mistakes are equal. Understanding the types helps you prioritize what to fix:

TypeELO CostFix
Hanging a piece-200CCT scan
Missing a tactic-150CCT scan
One-move blunder-100Blunder check
Positional mistake-30Study + experience
Opening inaccuracy-20Opening knowledge

Fix the top three first. At ELO 0-1000, 90% of losses come from the top three. The CCT scan addresses all of them.

Use this on EVERY move:

  1. Opponent's last move — what changed? What does that piece now attack? What was it defending? (3 seconds)
  2. CCT Scan: Checks, Captures, Threats — your turn.
  3. Choose your move. Select a candidate. STOP. Don't play it yet.
  4. Blunder Check: Is the destination safe? What am I leaving undefended? What's their best reply? Am I walking into a discovered attack? (5 seconds)
  5. Play the move.

This protocol takes 10 seconds and will save more games than any other single habit.

Pattern 1: "I didn't see the bishop"

A long-range bishop or queen on the other side of the board captures your piece.

Fix: Trace the diagonals from every enemy bishop and queen before moving.

Pattern 2: "Knight forks everywhere"

King and queen get forked by an enemy knight.

Fix: Check if any enemy knight can reach a fork square (especially one that attacks king + queen).

blocks.caption: Black's knight on c6 could jump to e5 (forking queen + f7) or d4 (forking queen + c2). Always scan for knight fork squares before moving your queen!

blocks.html:
Pattern 3: "I captured the wrong thing"

You saw a free piece and captured it — but it was a trap.

Fix: If a capture looks too easy, take 10 extra seconds. Ask "Why is this free?"

Pattern 4: "I forgot about pins"

Moved a pinned piece, exposing the king or queen.

Fix: Check alignment of enemy bishops/rooks/queens with your king and queen.

Pattern 5: "Back rank checkmate"

Castled king with no luft gets mated on the back rank.

Fix: Play h3/h6 or g3/g6 early enough to create an escape square.

Two Types of Players at ELO 0-1000:

  1. The Speed Demon: Plays every move in 2 seconds. Blunders by move 15.
  2. The Overthinker: Spends 3 minutes on move 4. Time trouble by move 25.

Time Budgeting (10-minute game):

PhaseMovesBudget
Opening1-81 minute
Early middlegame9-203 minutes
Late middlegame21-353 minutes
Endgame36+3 minutes

Critical moves deserve more time: capture sequences, material-changing moves, king danger.

The Time Check Habit: Every 5 moves, glance at the clock. "Too fast? Too slow?"

Tilt Management

You just blundered your queen. What now?

Wrong response: "I'm an idiot." Makes 5 more bad moves and loses.

Right response: Deep breath. Stand up. Close your eyes for 10 seconds. The game isn't over — your opponent still has to prove they can win.

Don't Resign

At ELO 0-1000: Opponents stalemate when up 15 points, opponents flag in winning positions, opponents hang their queen back. Unless you're facing unavoidable checkmate, don't resign.

The "One Good Move" Mindset

You don't need 40 perfect moves. You need to play fewer bad moves than your opponent. Focus on not blundering, and their blunders will come.

sections.title: 6.6 Game Review — Fastest Path to Improvement

blocks.html:
The 5-Minute Review Protocol

After each game:

  1. Find the decisive mistake. Where did the evaluation swing? Why did you play that move?
  2. Replay from that position. What should you have played?
  3. Identify the pattern. Hanging piece? Missed tactic? Write it down.
  4. Set a goal for next game. "Next game, I will scan for enemy bishop diagonals."

Playing 100 games without reviewing is nearly useless. Playing 10 games and reviewing each deeply is how you improve.

Daily Routine (30-45 minutes)

ActivityTimePurpose
Tactics puzzles15 minPattern recognition, CCT practice
One serious game10-15 minApply principles
Review your game5-10 minLearn from mistakes
Study a concept5-10 minRotate through chapters

Milestones

ELOWhat You Should Be Able to Do
200Set up board, move all pieces, know values
400Control center, develop, castle, spot forks
600Run CCT scan automatically, avoid blunders
800Play a sound opening, win basic endgames
1000Convert winning positions, manage time, review games

Stage 0: Beginner Tactics (800-1400 puzzles)

Focus on basic tactical patterns: forks, pins, skewers. Run the CCT scan on every puzzle. Don't skip the CCT phases! Goal: 15 puzzles correctly.

Stage 1: Tactical Sharpness (1400-1650)

Longer sequences, complex patterns. Practice calculating 2-3 moves ahead. Goal: 20 puzzles.

Training Tips

  • Accuracy over speed. 5 puzzles at 100% > 20 at 25%.
  • Use slow mode. Complete full CCT analysis each time.
  • Review wrong attempts. Understand WHY it was wrong.

Chess is a journey, not a destination. ELO 1000 is not the end — it's the beginning of real chess understanding.

Three things to remember:

  1. CCT on every move. Checks, Captures, Threats. Make it automatic.
  2. Blunder check before you move. 5 seconds. It's your insurance policy.
  3. Review your games. Each game has one lesson. Find it.

The player who makes the fewest mistakes wins. Be that player.

Good luck. See you at 1000.

summary:
SkillAction
Blunder check5-step protocol on every move
Common patternsBishop diagonals, knight forks, pins, back rank
Time managementBudget your time; spend it on critical moves
PsychologyDon't resign, manage tilt
Game review5-minute protocol after every game
CCT practiceTactical trainer, 15 mins/day
